UX Case Study Mobile App HealthTech Peer to Peer Mindwave Solutions

Medical Share

A peer to peer platform that connects people who need medical equipment with those who have it — designed so that every user can act as both a borrower and a lender from a single app.

Role
End to End UX Designer
Duration
2 Months
Company
Mindwave Solutions
Tools
Figma · Notion · Google Docs
Platform
iOS and Android
Status
In Development

A full end to end UX project at Mindwave Solutions — from discovery through dev ready Figma handoff. Sole designer on the project, collaborating closely with the PM, business owners, and engineering team throughout.

Overview

What is Medical Share?

Medical Share is a mobile platform that connects people who need medical equipment with those who have it. Whether someone needs a ventilator for a week of home recovery or a wheelchair for a month of rehabilitation, Medical Share lets them rent from real people nearby — affordably and with confidence.

The platform's defining feature is dual role flexibility. Any user can list their own equipment as a lender on the same day they borrow something from someone else. This peer to peer model dramatically increases equipment availability and creates an earning opportunity for people who own medical devices that would otherwise sit idle.

🎯
My Role
End to end UX, discovery through dev ready Figma handoff
👥
Team
Project Manager, Business Owners, and Development Team
📱
Users
Borrowers and Lenders — one app, two distinct flows
🚚
Unique Feature
Middleman system for verified equipment pickup and delivery
Design Process

The Double Diamond.

I followed the Double Diamond (4D) framework throughout this project, ensuring I fully understood the problem space before moving into solutions. The framework kept every phase grounded in evidence rather than assumption.

01
Discover
Business goals and market research
02
Define
Personas and problem statement
03
Develop
Flows, wireframes, components
04
Deliver
UI design, prototype, usability testing
Phase 01 — Discover

Understanding the problem space.

The project manager provided initial requirements, but I wanted to understand the business goals more deeply before designing anything. I mapped out four core objectives that would guide every design decision throughout the project.

Increase Accessibility

Ensure users can easily find and rent or buy essential medical equipment near them, regardless of technical ability.

Simplify Transactions

Make the lending and borrowing process smooth and intuitive from the very first interaction.

Maximise Lender Revenue

Help lenders earn from idle equipment by giving them a structured and reliable platform to do so.

Build Trust

Create a secure environment where strangers can confidently share high-value medical equipment with each other.

Phase 01 — Secondary Research

Competitor analysis.

I used ChatGPT to quickly gather initial market landscape data, then validated and expanded those insights by using each competitor app directly. This two step approach ensured findings were grounded in real product experience rather than surface level summaries.

M
Medikabazaar
B2B medical supplies marketplace
Focus

B2B medical supplies catalog for hospitals and clinics

Strength

Extensive catalog of medical products across categories

Gap

Not built for individual borrowers — too complex for casual users

O
OLX Health Category
Peer to peer sales and rentals
Focus

General marketplace with a health listings category

Strength

Very wide user base across India

Gap

Serious trust and verification issues — dangerous for medical equipment

A
Apollo Home Care
Comprehensive healthcare services
Focus

Comprehensive healthcare services including equipment rentals

Strength

Trusted brand name and generally user-friendly navigation

Gap

Complex and friction-heavy onboarding for equipment rental specifically

P
PrimedQ
Medical equipment sales and rentals
Focus

Medical equipment sales and rentals with a search first experience

Strength

Easy search functionality and low learning curve

Gap

Limited visual appeal and no personalisation — feels generic and untrustworthy

Market Gap

No dedicated platform focused on peer to peer medical equipment rental existed. Every competitor either targeted B2B buyers, lacked trust and verification mechanisms, or made the rental process unnecessarily complex for everyday people. Medical Share had a clear and unoccupied space to own.

How Might We

Framing the opportunity.

To move from research findings to design opportunities, I developed four How Might We questions that guided every major decision in the project.

1

How might we simplify the process of renting and lending medical equipment so that anyone can complete a transaction confidently?

2

How might we build trust between borrowers and lenders so that people feel safe sharing high-value medical equipment with strangers?

3

How might we make pricing and availability fully transparent so that users can make informed decisions without contacting a lender first?

4

How might we create an intuitive platform that supports users acting as both borrowers and lenders without confusion between the two roles?

Phase 02 — Define

Who we are designing for.

Through stakeholder conversations and a deep analysis of the competitive landscape, two distinct user types emerged — each with different daily contexts, goals, and frustrations. Designing well for both without one undermining the other was the central structural challenge.

R
Ramesh Naidu
Borrower · 52 yrs · Hyderabad
Context

His father recently had a stroke and needs a wheelchair and oxygen concentrator for six weeks of home recovery. Hospital rentals are expensive and hard to arrange quickly.

Goals
  • Find available equipment nearby as quickly as possible
  • See clear pricing upfront with no hidden fees
  • Trust that equipment is genuine and in good condition
  • Arrange reliable pickup or delivery without hassle
Frustrations
  • OLX listings have no verification — risky for medical gear
  • Hospital rental desks are slow and often out of stock
  • No single platform shows availability, price, and location together
"I just need it today. I don't have time to call five different places."
S
Sunita Reddy
Lender · 38 yrs · Hyderabad
Context

She owns a ventilator and a hospital bed her mother used during recovery. They have been sitting unused for over a year. She wants to rent them out but has no reliable platform to do so.

Goals
  • List equipment quickly with minimal effort
  • Set her own price and availability window
  • Receive borrower requests and approve them with confidence
  • Track earnings and manage active rentals from one place
Frustrations
  • OLX is too generic with no medical-specific trust signals
  • No way to verify who is borrowing or why
  • No structured rental agreement or return tracking
"This equipment cost us a lot. I want to put it to use, but only with someone I can trust."
Refined Problem Statement

The core problem.

Problem Statement

Users struggle to find and rent essential medical equipment conveniently, and potential lenders lack a reliable platform to monetise their equipment efficiently.

User Needs Identified

  • Easy to use platform for both renting and lending
  • Clear upfront costs for rentals and purchases
  • Trustworthy environment with verified listings and user ratings

Market Gaps

  • No dedicated platform focused on peer to peer medical equipment rentals
  • Lack of trust signals and user friendly experience on existing platforms
Phase 03 — Develop

User flows and wireframes.

Before designing any screens I began mapping out interaction flows for different parts of the user journey. The main goal was to get the user started renting or buying an item without causing confusion, especially around the dual borrower and lender roles.

Low fidelity wireframes were shared with the PM and development team early so the structure could be validated before any visual investment. These wireframes also became the foundation for the component library that gave all 200 plus screens a consistent structure.

Low Fidelity · Quick start and product screens
200+Total Screens Designed
4DProcess Framework
2Distinct Role Flows
Phase 03 — Information Architecture

Restructuring the navigation.

The core structural challenge was designing a single app that serves two completely different roles without creating confusion. I restructured navigation to be role aware and task oriented before a single screen was designed.

Single navigation — all users see identical screens
→
Role aware navigation — borrower and lender sections clearly separated in Account
No location context on the home screen
→
Location shown at the top of Home — drives nearby listing relevance immediately
No way to request equipment not yet listed
→
Dedicated Request Item flow — notifies lenders within 25km automatically
Delivery coordination handled outside the app
→
Middleman system built in — verified third party handles pickup and drop off
No clear split between rented and purchased items
→
Borrowed Items screen with Rented and Bought tabs, distinct status indicators for each
Phase 04 — Deliver

Key screens and design decisions.

The visual design was built on a component library first — ensuring consistency across all screens before any individual flow was finalised. Screens are grouped below by the flow they belong to, matching the four flow groups from the project.

Home Screens

Borrower flow

This screen simplifies medical equipment access with location based listings, intuitive categories, and clear pricing. Easy navigation enhances user experience for quick borrowing, searching, and sharing.

Screen 01 — Home
The discovery hub
Location based listings and category navigation
Borrower

Design decisions

The home screen leads with the user's current location, immediately surfacing listings that are actually reachable. This single choice shapes the entire borrower experience: relevance over volume.

  • Category chips let users jump directly to an equipment type without searching, critical when someone needs something urgently
  • Popular Items and Nearby Items are two sections with distinct logic — one shows high demand equipment, the other prioritises distance
  • Each card shows price, rating, and location in a single glance, removing the need to open a listing just to evaluate it
  • The bottom nav gives equal visibility to Home, Borrow, Search, Share, and Account, reinforcing the dual role nature without hierarchy

Borrow Items

Borrower flow

These screens streamline the borrowing process by enabling users to search for medical equipment, request unavailable items, and get notified when they are added. Location based lender notifications and easy borrowing actions enhance accessibility.

Screen 02 — Item Detail
Everything needed to make a decision
Product detail with condition, pricing, and persistent CTA
Borrower

Design decisions

This screen is designed to close the trust gap that peer to peer platforms typically fail at. Rather than a plain image and a price, it surfaces everything a borrower needs to feel confident before committing.

  • Multiple thumbnail images let borrowers inspect equipment from different angles
  • Condition badge (New, Used, Refurbished) is visually prominent — medical equipment users care about this far more than typical marketplace users
  • Price and rental type (Rent vs Selling) are always visible, never buried
  • A persistent Borrow Now CTA stays in view as the user scrolls, reducing friction at the moment of intent
Screen 03 — Request Item
When what you need is not yet listed
Notify lenders within 25km automatically
Borrower

Design decisions

This screen addresses one of the most painful gaps found in research: a borrower knows what they need, but no one has listed it yet. The Request Item flow solves this without sending the user away empty-handed.

  • The borrower specifies equipment name, condition, quantity, type (Rent or Buy), and drop location, giving lenders everything they need to decide if they can fulfil the request
  • Once submitted, lenders within 25km are notified automatically with no manual coordination needed
  • Image upload is optional but encouraged, helping lenders identify exactly what model is needed for specialised equipment
Screen 04 — Map Search
Finding equipment by location
Live map view combined with nearby item list
Borrower

Design decisions

The Search screen combines a live map view with a filtered list below, giving users two ways to orient themselves depending on how they think about proximity.

  • The map occupies the top half of the screen — borrowers can visually gauge distance before selecting a listing, especially important for bulky equipment requiring in-person collection
  • Address, city, and zip code search in one field keeps input simple
  • Nearby Items below the map mirror the home screen logic but reflect the searched location, creating a consistent pattern across the app

Share Item Screens

Lender flow

These screens simplify the item sharing process by allowing users to list products for rent or sale, set prices, and target consumers or middlemen. Successful submissions confirm the addition, ensuring a seamless sharing experience.

Screen 05 — Share (Listing)
Listing equipment in minutes
Lender inventory management and new listing flow
Lender

Design decisions

The Share screen shows the lender their existing listed items and makes it easy to add more. The grid layout creates a familiar marketplace style experience that reduces the learning curve for new lenders.

  • Item count is surfaced prominently so lenders can quickly assess active inventory without drilling into each listing
  • Large imagery rather than text heavy rows reflects how lenders think about their physical equipment
  • Adding a new item follows a structured multi step form (name, condition, quantity, type, price, location) to ensure listings have enough detail for borrowers to make confident decisions

Borrowing and Lending Management

Both roles

These screens provide a comprehensive borrowing and lending management system. Users can track borrowed and bought items, view order statuses, and manage requests effectively. The cancellation flow is streamlined with instant feedback for a seamless experience.

Screen 06 — Borrowed Items
Tracking active and past rentals
Rented and Bought tabs with colour coded status indicators
Borrower

Design decisions

This screen consolidates a borrower's full rental history and active orders in one place. The Rented and Bought tabs create a clear separation between two fundamentally different transaction types.

  • Each row shows equipment image, name, rental period, status, and cost — a full picture without needing to open the detail view
  • Status communicated through both text and colour coded icons (green check for Confirmed, red cross for Cancelled) with no ambiguity even for users who scan quickly
  • Filter button at the bottom right lets users filter by status without cluttering the header area
Screen 07 — Order Confirmed
Post confirmation state and next steps
Confirmed order with track and cancel actions
Borrower

Design decisions

Once a borrower's request is confirmed, this screen gives full context on what happens next. For medical equipment, the post confirmation moment is high anxiety — users need to know the equipment is coming and when.

  • Green confirmation badge in the top right provides immediate visual reassurance
  • Selling Price is prominently displayed so there is no doubt about the final amount
  • Track Order and Cancel Item as two clear bottom actions — giving borrowers control without overwhelming them with options
  • Chat button in the lower right lets borrowers message the lender directly, critical for coordinating delivery of large equipment
Screen 08 — Borrower Requests
Managing incoming requests efficiently
Grouped by day with single tap approval
Lender

Design decisions

This screen is the lender's operational hub, showing all incoming borrower requests sorted by day. The design prioritises scan speed since lenders may receive multiple requests and need to triage quickly.

  • Requests grouped by Today and Yesterday create a temporal anchor that helps lenders prioritise without reading dates carefully
  • Each card shows borrower name, date, type, location, item, and quantity in a compact two row format
  • The plus button acts as the primary approval action so acceptance is a single tap
  • Item Requests and Return Requests are in separate tabs — each requires a completely different mental model
Screen 09 — Refer and Earn
Building the supply side through incentives
Referral programme for growing the lender network
Growth

Design decisions

Medical Share's growth depends on lenders — without equipment supply, borrowers have nothing to find. The Refer and Earn screen is a deliberate growth mechanic designed to onboard more lenders through existing users.

  • Total Earnings and Total Referrals on a prominent card make the programme feel rewarding and worth tracking
  • Two reward options (5% commission for a year, or flat ₹500 cash) let users choose the model that matches their goals
  • Referral code with one tap Copy Code action removes friction from the sharing moment
Phase 04 — Usability Study

Testing with real users.

I conducted a moderated usability study with colleagues including engineers and developers to gather feedback on the app's functionality, user experience, and overall usability. The study was designed to identify pain points, assess navigation ease, and gather concrete suggestions for improvement.

🔍

Objective 01 — Navigation Ease

Assess how smoothly users navigate between borrowing, lending, and account management sections without getting lost between the two roles.

📋

Objective 02 — Item Management

Understand how intuitive it is to add, borrow, sell, and manage items — including how clearly the difference between renting and buying is communicated.

✅

Objective 03 — Overall Satisfaction

Gauge how satisfied users are with the design, layout, and responsiveness of the app — particularly around confirmation states and visual feedback.

Key Findings

⚠️

Rented vs Bought Confusion

Users found it unclear to differentiate between rented and bought items due to similar layouts and visual cues. Design iterations were made after the study to strengthen the visual distinction between the two states.

👍

Clear Item Management

Adding, viewing, and managing items was rated as intuitive. Users appreciated the organised structure and clear categorisation throughout the listing and browsing flows.

💬

High Satisfaction with Visual Feedback

Users appreciated confirmation messages and visual cues like checkmarks, which reassured them that their actions were successfully completed without any explanation needed.

Reflection

What I learned.

Medical Share was my first end to end mobile UX project in a professional setting. Working alongside a PM, business owners, and a development team in a real product environment taught me things that no academic project could have.

01

Dual role UX is a structural problem first

The hardest design work happened in the IA phase, not the UI phase. Getting the navigation structure right so that borrower and lender flows never bleed into each other determined everything downstream.

02

Trust is a design material

In medical equipment rentals, every UI decision either builds or erodes trust. Condition badges, borrower verification fields, and structured request details are not features — they are trust infrastructure.

03

Testing with engineers is underrated

My usability participants were developers, not end users. Despite that, their technical instincts surfaced real interaction problems, particularly around state ambiguity in the Borrowed Items screen.

04

What I would do differently

With more time, I would have conducted interviews with actual patients or caregivers renting equipment for home recovery. Secondary research told me about market gaps, but first person stories would have sharpened the emotional depth of the personas considerably.
